questions.question: A deductive argument is 'valid' when:
options: IF the premises are true, the conclusion MUST be true (logical form is correct)
questions.explanation: Validity is about FORM, not truth. 'All fish can fly. Salmon is a fish. Therefore salmon can fly.' — this is VALID (form is correct) but not SOUND (premise is false). Sound = valid + true premises.

questions.question: The ad hominem fallacy attacks the argument's logic rather than the person making it.
questions.explanation: Ad hominem attacks the PERSON, not the argument. 'You can't trust his climate research — he drives an SUV.' The person's character/behavior doesn't invalidate their evidence. Attack the data/logic, not the source.

questions.question: Modus ponens: If P then Q. P is true. Therefore ___ is true.
questions.explanation: Modus ponens: P→Q, P, ∴Q. (If it rains, streets are wet. It rains. Therefore streets are wet.) Modus tollens: P→Q, ¬Q, ∴¬P. (Streets are dry, therefore it's not raining.)

questions.question: Match each logical fallacy to its example:

right: Misrepresenting someone's position to make it easier to attack
Presenting only two options when more exist
'Einstein believed in God, so God must exist'
'If we allow X, next Y will happen, then Z — catastrophe!'

questions.explanation: Straw man: argue against a weaker version of the position. False dichotomy: either/or when a spectrum exists. Appeal to authority: expertise in one field doesn't transfer to all. Slippery slope: unwarranted chain of consequences.

questions.question: Evaluate this argument: 'Millions of people believe in astrology. Therefore, astrology must have some truth to it.'

scenario.context: A friend cites astrology's popularity as evidence of its validity.

options: Valid — popular beliefs usually have some basis in reality
Fallacious — argumentum ad populum (appeal to popularity). Popularity doesn't equal truth.
Partially valid — where there's smoke there's fire
Can't be evaluated without knowing astrology's actual evidence
questions.explanation: Argumentum ad populum: 'Many believe X, therefore X is true.' Millions believed the Earth was flat, the sun orbited Earth, and bloodletting cured disease. Truth is determined by evidence, not vote count.

questions.question: Is it ethical to use logical fallacies deliberately in persuasion if the cause is just? (e.g., emotional appeals to fund cancer research)
questions.scenario: A charity deliberately uses emotional manipulation (showing suffering children) rather than statistics. The cause is legitimate — they save lives. But they're bypassing rational evaluation.

options.label: Justified — lives saved outweigh the minor 'harm' of emotional manipulation
options.framework: Consequentialist — ends justify means if outcome is good

options.label: Never ethical — respect for rational autonomy means only valid arguments should persuade
options.framework: Kantian — people shouldn't be manipulated, even for good causes

options.label: Acceptable for awareness, not for specific claims — emotion draws attention, facts should convince
The line is consent — if people know they're being emotionally appealed to, it's fine

questions.explanation: Rhetoric has always combined emotion (pathos) and logic (logos). The ethical standard: use emotion to engage attention and concern, then provide honest evidence for the specific claim. Emotion without facts is manipulation; facts without emotion are ignored.
